user_prompt: question, is VBUS a GPIO? point out where it is initialized as an input
agent_reply: No, `VBUS` here is not an ESP32 GPIO. It’s a power/PMIC signal being read over I2C through M5Unified.
For Core2 / AXP192, VBUS is read from PMIC registers:
The nearest thing to “initialized as input” is not a GPIO input setup. It’s PMIC ADC enable:
- AXP192: `0x82, 0xFF // reg82h ADC all on` in [Power_Class.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/.pio/libdeps/thefly_bringuptest/M5Unified/src/utility/Power_Class.cpp:464)
- AXP2101 Core2 v1.1: `0x30, 0x0F // ADC enabled` in [Power_Class.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/.pio/libdeps/thefly_bringuptest/M5Unified/src/utility/Power_Class.cpp:534)
So: no `pinMode(VBUS, INPUT)` exists because VBUS is not exposed to the ESP32 as a normal GPIO in this path. It’s PMIC telemetry.

user_prompt: We will work on `lib\FlyGuiViews\RecordingView`, I've created some files inside with names that indicate their purpose
`RecordingView.cpp` itself will be used in two ways, one is for a call/meeting recording over bluetooth, and the other is a local memo recording
For a call/meeting, the speaker button is available, and there is a "answer call" button available.

For a local memo recording, there is no speaker button, and also no "answer call", but there is a button that can change the memo type (for things like: todo, journal, idea, etc). Cycle through the `sprit_memotype_` sprites
Both of these modes use the same layout.

There is always a red border, use four filled rectangles for this, the border is 10 pixels thick, drawing a box starting from x=0 y=10 (because we still want to see the top bar, do not move the top bar) down to the bottom right corner

Along the bottom of the screen, we now have 300 pixel wide area to work with because of two 10px thick borders, this is divided into three 100x100 pixel buttons. These buttons are also linked to the dedicated buttons `BtnA` `BtnB` and `BtnC`.

`AudioDeviceButton` can either be for a mic or a speaker. For a mic button, touching it will activate the mic for push-to-talk, touching it again will cause it to go back to speaker mode. The mic icon will be fully bright when in mic mode, and faded, with a red X draw over it when not in mic mode. For a speaker button, it is faded when in mic mode, and not faded when in speaker mode. There is also a speaker muted mode that is the not-faded button but with a red X drawn over it. When in speaker mode, touching the speaker button toggles speaker mute.
The drawing of the red X is a shared feature, draw the `sprit_muted_x` over the center of the button, it's a square so don't need to care about opacity.

Another shared feature: The top row of pixels is a audio level meter reflecting the peak levels. The line grows from the center outwards. When the peak level is at saturation, the entire row of pixels is fully red. When the level is 0, no pixels show. A low level is a short line in the center that is white. The colour reflects the level, Red is fully saturated, white is low or 0, yellow is in the middle.

The first of the three buttons is the mic button `sprit_btn_mic`, then the speaker button `sprit_btn_speaker` (if required), the third one will be a exit button `sprit_btn_end` that ends the recording.
The first coordinate is x=10 y=130, the second is x=110 y=130, the third is x=210 y=130
`RecordingView` is the owner of the code that changes the state of the buttons, not the buttons themselves
For the answer call button or the memo type button, the coordinate is x=260 y=70, size is 50x50, use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`
If in bluetooth mode, there will be a static non-interactive sprite at x=260 y=20 that is also 50x50, use `sprit_bluetooth_50`
The icon inside "answer call" button changes according to `PhoneUiState CallManager::uiState()`. use `sprit_pickup` and `sprit_pickupdown`

Starting from x=14 y=101, there will be several lines of text. The first line is the recording file name, this is static. The second line should show how long the recording has been going on for, which needs the `lib\FlyGui\FlyGuiText.cpp`, the third line also uses `FlyGuiText` and shows all `getCallerInfoAt` items, one item at a time, cycling to the next one every 3 seconds.
For all the functions that need to control (so I don't mean just using getters) other modules like AudioManager or AudioFileRecorder or BtManager or etc, use `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp`
This is a long instruction, warn me about mistakes
agent_reply: - Added Bluetooth vs memo modes with shared layout, red border, bottom button mapping, caller-info cycling, elapsed time, filename text, and center-out audio meter.
- Added `RecordingViewCallbacks.cpp` for external control of `AudioManager`, `AudioFileRecorder`, and `BtManager`.
- Replaced the old registered `ActiveRecordingView` with the new `RecordingView` in `src/all_init.cpp`.

user_prompt: When MainScreenView is loaded, it seems like the smartphone and laptop icons are faded until much later when something realizes they are available in the JSON, why is that? I thought the BtHostList loaded before the splash screen ends
I think the constructor `MainScreenView` is called way too early? Confirm this, I might instruct you later to allocate the views instead
agent_reply: Yes, confirmed: `MainScreenView` is constructed way too early for `bt_host_list`.
4. At that moment, global `bt_host_list` is still `nullptr`, so this evaluates as missing:
So yes, the constructor initially fades smartphone and laptop.
Small nuance: `BtHostList` is **not** loaded before the splash screen is shown. `setup()` does:
So the splash is displayed first, then the JSON is loaded while splash is still the current view.

That said, `MainScreenView::onLoad()` and `MainScreenView::redraw()` both call `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, so by the time `FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN` is shown after JSON load, it *should* correct itself. If it visibly stays faded until later, then either:
- the actual `/bluetooth.json` on SD does not have `"icon": "phone"` / `"icon": "laptop"` as expected,
- `loadFromMicroSd()` is failing or loading later than we think,
- or the redraw order/dirty state is not repainting the changed faded state immediately.

But your core suspicion is right: the constructor is absolutely too early to consult runtime-loaded config. Allocating views after config load, or moving all config-dependent sync into `onLoad()` only, would be cleaner.

user_prompt: if `gui->showView(FLYGUI_VIEW_MAIN)` happens after `bt_host_list->loadFromMicroSd()`, why does it seem like it took time for the phone and laptop items to load?
agent_reply: Yes, your instinct is right, but there’s a second thing making it look delayed.

`MainScreenView` is constructed early as a static in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:32), before `setup()` creates and loads `bt_host_list`. Its constructor calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and at that point `bt_host_list == nullptr`, so phone/laptop start faded.
But the bigger issue is this: [SplashView.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:31) automatically calls:
after the splash delay. That happens inside `show_splash()`, which currently runs before:
So the main view is actually shown once before the Bluetooth JSON is loaded.

`FlyGui::showView()` sees that main is already the current view and returns immediately without `onLoad()` or forced redraw. Then a later normal poll/redraw finally runs `MainScreenView::redraw()`, calls `syncBluetoothHostButtonFades()`, and the icons catch up.

So the real cause is not just early construction. It’s that `SplashView` hands off to main too early, and the later explicit `showView(MAIN)` becomes a no-op. Best fix later: remove the splash view’s automatic transition and let `setup()` be the only place that transitions to main after all boot data is loaded.

agent_reply: Your Bluetooth scan-mode change is valid: `ESP_BT_CONNECTABLE, ESP_BT_NON_DISCOVERABLE` is the right idle/default state if you want known hosts to connect while keeping normal discovery closed. user_prompt: I know you just deleted it, but we need a `WifiApModeView` that is a `FlyGuiView`. This will display Wi-Fi credentials while in soft AP mode.
Place `sprit_wifi_50` in the top left corner (but not covering the top bar). If this is the default super safe randomly generated soft AP, then also put `sprite_security_50` beside it.

The bottom right corner, also linked to `BtnC`, put in a clickable `FlyGuiItem` that switches between `sprit_eye_closed_100` and `sprit_eye_open_100` when touched. Open eye means the info on the screen is displayed, closed means the sensitive parts are displayed as "**********"
Below the Wi-Fi icon, there should be 5 lines of text, similar to below
IP: <IP of the internal web server, probably 192.168.4.1>
Below the IP line, use the smallest font, show a line of text that cycles through a few displayed data items, cycling every 3 seconds, the items are:
* "page loads: n", number of times the "/get_info" request has been served since starting the AP
* "logins: n", number of times the "/get_cfg" request has been served since starting the AP, it is not displayed under security-level 0
* "saves: n", number of times the "/set_cfg" request (also the reset password and reset memory) has been served since starting the AP
* "errors: n", number of times there has been an apparent error, such as security-level mismatch error, or, some form of authentication error, those are the important ones
* "downloads: n", number of times a file has been downloaded, also track deletions and uploads with this number even though it doesn't make sense
`WifiManager` needs getters for these, and they need to be reset when the Wi-Fi starts, there may be request URL handlers missing but we'll implement those later

I am aware that I did not include some sort of stop button. The user must either click the physical reset button or power button to exit. So add a red line of text, small, as the next line, hinting so. I have no idea if it fits on the screen but do it so I can see.
agent_reply: - Shows `sprit_wifi_50` at the top-left below the top bar.
- Shows `sprit_security_50` beside it for the generated secure AP.
- Shows SSID/password/IP, with SSID/password hidden as `**********` by default.
- Adds the bottom-right eye toggle using `sprit_eye_closed_100` / `sprit_eye_open_100`, touchable and also tied to `BtnC`.
- Cycles the small stats line every 3 seconds.
- Adds the red “reset or power off” exit hint.
- Added “Default Secure AP” to the Wi-Fi scroll list.
- Selecting it starts `WifiManager::startGeneratedSoftAp()` and shows the AP view.
- Selecting a configured AP starts that AP and shows the same view.
- Added `WifiManager` web counter getters and reset-on-AP-start behavior.
- Counted `/get_info`, `/get_cfg`, `/set_cfg`, reset password/memory, downloads/deletes/uploads, and notable errors.

- `pio run -e thefly_sec0` and `pio run -e thefly_sec1` both compile the changed files successfully, then still fail at final link with the existing IRAM overflow: `iram0_0_seg` overflowed by `2152 bytes`.

user_prompt: The SplashView screen should also show both the BDADDR and Wi-Fi MAC address in the smallest font, just make it say "B: <addr>" and "W: <addr>", use hyphens within the address between the bytes
And in the bottom right corner of the screen, in a larger font, show 4 hex digits of the tamper-evidence-code, if the security-level is exactly 2
The device information dialog should also show 4 hex digits of the tamper-evidence-code, if the security-level is 2 or higher
agent_reply: - level-2-only tamper code in the bottom-right, using a larger font.
- [src/main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:116>) refreshes the splash overlay again after Wi-Fi config loads, so the tamper code has the cached network hash available.
- [src/main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:705>) appends `Tamper: XXXX` to info dialogs for security level 2+.
- [lib/FlyGuiViews/ModalDialog.h](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/ModalDialog.h:39>) bumps dialog text capacity so the extra line has room.
Verification: `pio run -e thefly_sec2` compiled the touched files and reached link, then failed on the existing IRAM limit: `.iram0.text` overflowed by 2232 bytes.

user_prompt: when a `ScrollItem` for a cloud upload destination is triggered, run `CloudUpload::start`

before even that, there should be a callback configured, and it should be configured for a `ModalDialog` with an appropriate icon, thumbs up for success, error for failure. Dismissing the dialog causes the microcontroller to reboot
inside `lib\FlyGuiViews` create a `CloudUploadView` that inherits `ConnWaitingView`, but with a progress update function. It uses the sprite `sprit_cloudupload_100`
during the upload, meaning immediately after `CloudUpload::start` returns true, show the `CloudUploadView`. The cancel button of the `CloudUploadView` causes the `CloudUpload` to cancel.

`CloudUploadView` needs a progress bar, it is 4 pixels below the logo sprite, it is 300 pixels wide and 12 pixels tall, centered. To draw it, draw the frame borders as H-lines and V-lines, then draw a filled rectangle with a color (use HSV where hue for red is 0% progress and hue for green is 50% progress, and not let it exceed the hue for green) with a width representing percentage within the 300 pixels, then the rest is also filled with a black rectangle.

4 pixels below that, use text to say the percentage (do not use decimal places), and then how many files out of total files have been uploaded, include how many have failed, it might look like "12% 5/10 (5 errs)". Use a normal font centered on the screen, but if it gets too long for the screen width then shrink to the smallest font and start from x=1.
the progress drawing should be a part of `updateHourglass`
percentage is bytes transfered vs total bytes
agent_reply: Implemented the cloud upload flow and progress view.
- Cloud upload scroll items now call `CloudUpload::start`.
- The completion callback is configured before starting upload.
- Successful upload shows a thumbs-up `ModalDialog`; failure/cancel shows warning/error. Dismissing the dialog reboots the device.
- Progress bar and progress text update through `updateHourglass`.
- Progress color moves HSV red to green, capped at green.
- Initial `pio run -e thefly_sec0` compiled but hit IRAM0 overflow.
- Sterile rebuild succeeded and produced `firmware.bin`, `firmware.elf`, and `firmware.map`.

user_prompt: read the flow diagram in `developer-docs\ui-design.md` and compare it against the current implementation, there will be things not implemented, and things maybe implemented differently
agent_reply: I compared `developer-docs/ui-design.md` against the current FlyGui/main flow. The short version: the broad skeleton exists, but the implementation has collapsed several diagram nodes into one reusable `ScrollView` plus `ModalDialog`, and the Cloud/Wi-Fi-connected path is much less complete than the diagram implies.
- Boot to Splash to Main exists. Splash stays up until touch: [SplashView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:20>).
- Top bar is global in `FlyGui::drawTopBar`, with date/time, battery, and disk warning: [FlyGui.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:665>).
- Main screen has common actions: Bluetooth, info, memo, phone/laptop quick connect, Wi-Fi: [MainScreenView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp:32>).
- Recording has the thick red tally border: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:447>).
- Memo recording exists and can cycle memo type while recording: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:289>).
- Bluetooth connect/pair/info/delete are effectively implemented through the Bluetooth `ScrollView` context and dialogs.
- Wi-Fi AP mode exists as `WifiApModeView`, with credentials and live AP stats.
- Recording stop goes to a finished confirmation dialog, then Main: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:257>).

- `Bluetooth`, `WiFi`, and `Cloud` are not separate concrete screen classes. They are mostly contexts inside one `ScrollView`; the enum still has older-style placeholders like `FLYGUI_VIEW_BLUETOOTH`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WIFI`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WEB_ACTION`, etc., but those views are not registered in `init_gui`: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:110>).
- `BTConnect` and `BTPair` are both represented by `ConnWaitingView` modes, not separate view classes: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:180>).
- `InfoScreen`, `BTInfo`, `WiFiInfo`, `BTErrorDialog`, `WifiErrorDialog`, `CloudErrorDialog`, and finished confirmations are all `ModalDialog` with different text/icons/return views.
- `BTUnpairConfirm` is implemented as ScrollView delete mode plus a modal confirmation, returning to the same Bluetooth scroll context.

- Cloud upload completion always reboots after dismissing the completion/failure dialog, matching the diagram’s success path but also doing it for failure completion: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:877>).

- `WiFiConnect --> established --> Cloud` is not currently wired. `scanAndConnect()` starts scan/connect, but once connected there is no callback registered to populate/show the Cloud scroll context. `populateCloud()` exists, but I don’t see it called anywhere.
- Selecting an individual Wi-Fi station does nothing right now: `onclick_wifi_station` only logs: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:638>).
- `Cloud` as a user-visible menu is not reachable from the current flow, despite `ScrollView::populateCloud()` existing.
- `CloudEnroll` and `CloudEnrollConfirmation` are not implemented as GUI flows.
- `PasswordReset` and `MemoryReset` are web/AP administration features, not on-device GUI screens. The AP view intentionally has no stop/reset button except physical reset/power.
- `WiFiAP --> password reset/memory reset --> Boot` is therefore not a FlyGui transition.
- `BTConnect --> cancel --> Main` is slightly different: cancel returns to the remembered previous view, often the Bluetooth scroll, not always Main.
- `BTErrorDialog --> Main` is often `BTErrorDialog --> Scroll`, because connection errors return to the Bluetooth list.
- “Change volume during actual calls” does not appear implemented in the recording UI. There is mic/speaker mode and speaker mute behavior, but not volume adjustment.
- “Mute or unmute mic” is only partially aligned; the UI toggles audio mode and speaker mute, not a clear mic mute/unmute control.

Incoming Bluetooth connection to Recording is substantially implemented through pending flags and `on_bluetooth_state_changed`/recording handling. It applies broadly, with special suppression during pairing confirmation and with Wi-Fi AP activation shutting Bluetooth down, which matches the “except when Wi-Fi radio owns the radio” intent.

I compared `developer-docs/ui-design.md` against the current FlyGui/main flow. The short version: the broad skeleton exists, but the implementation has collapsed several diagram nodes into one reusable `ScrollView` plus `ModalDialog`, and the Cloud/Wi-Fi-connected path is much less complete than the diagram implies.
- Boot to Splash to Main exists. Splash stays up until touch: [SplashView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/SplashView.cpp:20>).
- Top bar is global in `FlyGui::drawTopBar`, with date/time, battery, and disk warning: [FlyGui.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGui/FlyGui.cpp:665>).
- Main screen has common actions: Bluetooth, info, memo, phone/laptop quick connect, Wi-Fi: [MainScreenView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/MainScreenView.cpp:32>).
- Recording has the thick red tally border: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:447>).
- Memo recording exists and can cycle memo type while recording: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:289>).
- Bluetooth connect/pair/info/delete are effectively implemented through the Bluetooth `ScrollView` context and dialogs.
- Wi-Fi AP mode exists as `WifiApModeView`, with credentials and live AP stats.
- Recording stop goes to a finished confirmation dialog, then Main: [RecordingView.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/FlyGuiViews/RecordingView/RecordingView.cpp:257>).

- `Bluetooth`, `WiFi`, and `Cloud` are not separate concrete screen classes. They are mostly contexts inside one `ScrollView`; the enum still has older-style placeholders like `FLYGUI_VIEW_BLUETOOTH`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WIFI`, `FLYGUI_VIEW_WEB_ACTION`, etc., but those views are not registered in `init_gui`: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:110>).
- `BTConnect` and `BTPair` are both represented by `ConnWaitingView` modes, not separate view classes: [all_init.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:180>).
- `InfoScreen`, `BTInfo`, `WiFiInfo`, `BTErrorDialog`, `WifiErrorDialog`, `CloudErrorDialog`, and finished confirmations are all `ModalDialog` with different text/icons/return views.
- `BTUnpairConfirm` is implemented as ScrollView delete mode plus a modal confirmation, returning to the same Bluetooth scroll context.

- Cloud upload completion always reboots after dismissing the completion/failure dialog, matching the diagram’s success path but also doing it for failure completion: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:877>).

- `WiFiConnect --> established --> Cloud` is not currently wired. `scanAndConnect()` starts scan/connect, but once connected there is no callback registered to populate/show the Cloud scroll context. `populateCloud()` exists, but I don’t see it called anywhere.
- Selecting an individual Wi-Fi station does nothing right now: `onclick_wifi_station` only logs: [main.cpp](</d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:638>).
- `Cloud` as a user-visible menu is not reachable from the current flow, despite `ScrollView::populateCloud()` existing.
- `CloudEnroll` and `CloudEnrollConfirmation` are not implemented as GUI flows.
- `PasswordReset` and `MemoryReset` are web/AP administration features, not on-device GUI screens. The AP view intentionally has no stop/reset button except physical reset/power.
- `WiFiAP --> password reset/memory reset --> Boot` is therefore not a FlyGui transition.
- `BTConnect --> cancel --> Main` is slightly different: cancel returns to the remembered previous view, often the Bluetooth scroll, not always Main.
- `BTErrorDialog --> Main` is often `BTErrorDialog --> Scroll`, because connection errors return to the Bluetooth list.
- “Change volume during actual calls” does not appear implemented in the recording UI. There is mic/speaker mode and speaker mute behavior, but not volume adjustment.
- “Mute or unmute mic” is only partially aligned; the UI toggles audio mode and speaker mute, not a clear mic mute/unmute control.

Incoming Bluetooth connection to Recording is substantially implemented through pending flags and `on_bluetooth_state_changed`/recording handling. It applies broadly, with special suppression during pairing confirmation and with Wi-Fi AP activation shutting Bluetooth down, which matches the “except when Wi-Fi radio owns the radio” intent.
